coredata.dc:title: Long wavelength emissive ZnO/CQDs phosphor with high color purity and its application in sensitive detection of cadmium (II)
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Islam, Shafi Ul
Latief, Urosa
Ali, Javid
Khan, Mohd. Shahid
coredata.dc:description: Production of color-tunable emission via simple method for upcoming displays and lighting technologies, and their exceptional luminescence provide considerable potential in sensing application. As environmental pollution and diseases brought by cadmium have always been global issues, therefore, it is imperative to develop an inexpensive, quick, easy, and selective approach for detecting trace cadmium. Herein, we describe the preperation of ZnO/carbon quantum dots (ZnO/CQDs) nanocomposite that emits strongly in the yellow-orange zone with the chromaticity coordinates (0.51, 0.47) and a high color purity (CP) value of 94.3%. The as-produced ZnO/CQDs are used as a fluorescence sensor for Cd2+ via quenching mechanism due to competitive coordination between ZnO/CQDs and Cd2+ with the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.14 μM. Additionally, the suggested method can successfully detect Cd2+ in water samples, demonstrating the feasibility and potential uses of the sensing platform. This study may pave the way for the development of environmentally safe, high-performing, and inexpensive color-tunable phosphor for white LEDs and sensing applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites
Photoluminescence
Phosphor
Fluorimetric sensor
White LEDs

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of ZnO and ZnO/CQDs nanostructures
Luminescence measurements for metal ion sensing
Determination of Cd2+ in real samples
Determination of Cd2+ in real water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of ZnO and ZnO/CQDs nanomaterials.
FESEM images and size distribution histogram of (a, b) ZnO (c,d) ZnO/CQDs nanomaterials (e, f) TEM image of CQDs and size distribution histogram.
FTIR spectra of ZnO and ZnO/CQDs nanomaterials.
XPS spectra of ZnO and ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite (a) survey of the sample (b) Zn 2p spectrum (c) O 1 s spectrum (d) C 1 s spectrum.
(a) UV–Visible spectra and (b) Tauc plot of ZnO and ZnO/CQDs nanomaterials.
PL spectra of (a) ZnO and ZnO/CQDs nanomaterials (b) CQDs (c) CIE diagram of ZnO and ZnO/CQDs nanomaterials.
Fluorescence emission intensity of ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites as a function of (a) different pH values (b) different ionic concentration (c) different incubation time.
Relative fluorescence emission intensity of ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites towards Cd2+ and other metal ions. The error bars stand for the three replicate measurements.

(a) Fluorescence spectra dependence of ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites upon addition of different concentration of Cd2+ (b) Linear calibration plot between fluorescence quenching efficiency and Cd2+ concentration in the scale of 0–60 μM. The error bars stand for the three replicate measurements.
A schematic diagram illustrating the quenching process when Cd2+ ions are added to the synthesized ZnO/CQDs.
(a) Zeta potential histogram of ZnO/CQDs and ZnO/CQDs + Cd2+ (b) FTIR spectra of ZnO/CQDs and ZnO/CQDs + Cd2+ (c) Lifetimes of steady-state fluorescence of ZnO/CQDs and ZnO/CQDs + Cd2+.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of the ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite.
The CIE (x, y) chromaticity coordinates, color corelated temperature (CCT) and color purity (CP) of as-synthesized nanomaterials.
Comparison of Cd2+ detection with earlier literature report.
Recovery outcomes of the detection of Cd2+ in water samples.

head.ce:title: Long wavelength emissive ZnO/CQDs phosphor with high color purity and its application in sensitive detection of cadmium (II)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Jamia Millia Islamia, New Delhi 110025, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Jamia Millia Islamia, New Delhi 110025, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Production of color-tunable emission via simple method for upcoming displays and lighting technologies, and their exceptional luminescence provide considerable potential in sensing application. As environmental pollution and diseases brought by cadmium have always been global issues, therefore, it is imperative to develop an inexpensive, quick, easy, and selective approach for detecting trace cadmium. Herein, we describe the preperation of ZnO/carbon quantum dots (ZnO/CQDs) nanocomposite that emits strongly in the yellow-orange zone with the chromaticity coordinates (0.51, 0.47) and a high color purity (CP) value of 94.3%. The as-produced ZnO/CQDs are used as a fluorescence sensor for Cd2+ via quenching mechanism due to competitive coordination between ZnO/CQDs and Cd2+ with the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.14 μM. Additionally, the suggested method can successfully detect Cd2+ in water samples, demonstrating the feasibility and potential uses of the sensing platform. This study may pave the way for the development of environmentally safe, high-performing, and inexpensive color-tunable phosphor for white LEDs and sensing applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: White light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) have supplanted traditional incandescent and fluorescent lights in recent years due to their superior luminous efficiency, reduced energy consumption, and durability [1]. A WLED is fabricated by combining light of several luminescent colors, primarily by combining blue and yellow light and tri-color (blue, green, and red) light [2]. An indium-containing indium gallium nitride (InGaN) blue chip paired with yellow phosphor is the most popular way to make WLEDs (YAG:Ce). Another approach uses a WLED system that combines a blue chip built on GaN with red and green phosphors [3,4]. In commercialised phosphors based on rare-earth ions, some nitride/oxynitride green phosphors have been employe++d, including SiAlON:Eu2+, MSi2O2N2:Eu20+ and Ba2SiO4:Eu2+ [5,6]. Recent advancements in electronics and technology have been greatly aided by several semiconductors with exceptional luminescence, such as ZnO, ZnS and TiO2. Among them, ZnO, has intrinsic defects produced during synthesis which are responsible for its luminescence in both the visible and UV regions. Six kinds of defects namely: oxygen interstitials (Oi), oxygen vacancies (Vo), oxygen antisites (Zno), zinc antisites (Ozn), zinc interstitials (Zni) and zinc vacancies (Vzn) are present in ZnO. These defects refer to imperfections or irregularities in the crystal structure of the material that naturally occur during its formation. Additionally, engineering O and Zn vacancies in this oxide might possibly be able to modify its photoluminescence emission [7]. In this way, by interacting with other carbon nanoparticles, its emission properties can be tuned. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a new class of carbon nanomaterials, have come to the forefront of attention due to their distinctive features [8,9]. Their synthesis primarily focuses on simple preparation methods, small particle sizes, surface passivation and functionalization, biocompatibility, high-temperature stability, photoluminescence (PL) capabilities, low toxicity and a chemically inert structure [10,11]. In relation to these, CQDs have already attracted interest in a variety of disciplines, including biomedical applications, WLEDs, photocatalysis, and sensors [12,13]. Additionally, their hybridization with ZnO has been reported as a fresh way to improve color performance and build phosphors that are used to create an emission spectrum that is both significantly broader and tunable.

Widiyandari et al. [14] recently examined the use of ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites for the degradation of pollutants, with an emphasis on their advancement. Despite the many reports on the CQDs for various applications, we investigate the synthesis of ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites, for the purpose of promoting the sensing of health hazardous heavy metal ions. In the current era, a major environmental problem on a global scale is pollution by heavy metal ions due to their dangerous effects and high toxicity on health of humans as well as other living organisms [8,15]. The kidneys, liver, and reproductive organs of humans are badly affected by cadmium ions (Cd2+), a very toxic heavy metal having teratogenic and carcinogenic properties [16,17]. Furthermore, due to their non-degradable qualities, cadmium is easily collected in the human body by means of the food chain. Consequently, it is essential to accurately and quickly monitor Cd2+ pollution in food samples in order to ensure public health and food safety. Currently, cadmium is detected using number of conventional techniques, including inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopy (ICPMS), electron paramagnetic resonance, immunoassay, and synchrotron radiation X-ray spectrometry [18–20]. However, the implementation of these approaches is constrained by the difficult sample preparation, simple analytical process, high cost and time consumption. Fluorescence spectrometry has therefore been extensively employed in the detection of heavy metal ions, particularly cadmium ions, because of its high sensitivity and selectivity, straightforward sample preparation, and low cost. Therefore, there is a great deal of enthusiasm for producing novel nanomaterials and formulating strategies for creating accurate and sensitive analytical techniques for quickly finding Cd2+ in the ecosystem. To the best of our knowledge, no previous research has been done on the use of longer wavelength emissive ZnO/ CQDs in a simple, affordable, alternative, and compatible manner as a fluorescence sensor for selective Cd2+ detection.

Here, we present our recent developments to enhance the optical characteristics of ZnO nanostructure by compositing with CQDs. ZnO/CQDs were designed as sensitive fluorimetric chemosensors for the purpose of determining Cd2+ in the range of 0 to 150 µM. Additionally, ZnO/CQDs exhibit exceptional color characteristics with a high CP value of 94.3%, CIE coordinates (0.51, 0.47), and a CCT value of 2513 K showing their potential for warm white LEDs. We argue that this process of producing solid-state luminous materials makes it possible to use the resulting nanocomposite in a wide range of applications, including white LEDs and sensing.

In the current work, Chemicals of analytical grade were used without further purification. Zinc acetate dihydrate (Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O, 98 %), was purchased from Alfaaesar. Methanol f(CH3OH, 99 %) was bought from Fisher Scientific. From Merck life science private limited, citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7·H2O, 99 %) was purchased. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) was bought from S.d.fine-chem Ltd. For all reactions, double distilled water was used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of ZnO and ZnO/CQDs nanostructures
ce:section.ce:para: First, zinc acetate dihydrate and methanol were employed to prepare ZnO nanorods. 8 g of zinc acetate dihydrate was dissolved in 56 ml of methanol and the solution was magnetically stirred for 10 min at room temperature. After 10 min, the temperature was increased to 80 0C. The resulting solution was subjected to gelation under constant stirring at 80 0C for 5 hrs, from which the powder was obtained. The resulting powder was annealed for 6 hrs at 450 0C and ZnO nanopowder was obtained.

Citric acid was used to prepare pure Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs). 0.768 g of Citric acid was dissolved in 20 ml of distilled water and 0.5 N NaOH solutions were used to maintain the pH at 6. Then the solution was put into Teflon-lined autoclave and hydrothermally treated for 10 hrs at 180 0C. Subsequently the reaction was completed, the autoclave was cooled at room temperature, and using the syringe filter 0.22 µM, the solution was filtered. Then the desired Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) were obtained.

The synthesis process of ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite was achieved as follows: 0.5 g of ZnO was dissolved in 30 ml of distilled water and for 30 min. stirred magnetically. Then the10 ml of CQDs solution was mixed with as-prepared ZnO solution followed by stirring continuously for 12 hrs. The precipitate was obtained by centrifugation followed by washing alternatively three times with distilled water and ethanol, and dried in an oven for 12 hrs at 100 0C. After that, the samples were heated for one hour in a muffle furnace at 250 °C. Thus, ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite was prepared. The preparation of the ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite is illustrated diagrammatically in Scheme 1 .

Several characterization techniques were used to characterize the prepared nanomaterials. FESEM images were taken with a Zeiss, Sigma HD Scanning Electron Microscope in order to study the surface morphology of the materials. To analyse the phase purity and crystalline character, the XRD was conducted using a Rigaku Smart Lab with a CuKα radiation source (=1.54059 Å). Using the FTIR Perkin Elmer Spectrum100 Spectrometer, various functional groups were determined. X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy was used to study the chemical composition and bonding present on the surface using a Versa Probe III (XPS) Surface Analysis Instrument. The optical characteristics were investigated using a double beam UV–vis spectrophotometer from LABMAN (Model, LMSP-UV1900). In order to record the photoluminescence spectra, an Agilent Cary Eclipse Spectrophotometer was used. Time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) equipment from FL920, Edinburgh Instruments, UK, was used to do measurements of time resolved photoluminescence (TRPL). The CIE 1931 diagram was used to determine the color-related properties.

Rhodamine B, with a quantum yield of 31% in water, served as a reference for calculating the quantum yield of the synthesized samples. Equation (1) was employed to ascertain the quantum yield of the synthesized samples in a water medium.
(1)
Q =
Q
′
.
I
I
′
.
A
′
A
.
η
η
′
2

In the given equation, Q and Q′ represent the QY (quantum yield) of synthesized samples and rhodamine B, respectively. I and I′ refer to the integrated area of the fluorescence emission peak (at a specified wavelength) of samples and rhodamine B. A and A′ correspond to the absorbance of synthesized samples and rhodamine B, respectively, measured at 340 nm. η (synthesized samples) and η′ (rhodamine B) denote the refractive indices of the solvent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescence measurements for metal ion sensing
ce:section.ce:para: Solutions containing various metal ions, namely CdCl2, Zn(NO3)2, Cu(NO3)2·10H2O, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, Bi(NO3)2·5H2O, Mg(NO3)2·6H2O, FeN3O9·9H2O, Sr(NO3)2, SnCl2, CoCl2·6H2O, MnCl2·4H2O, NaCl were prepared. These metal ion solutions, each with a concentration 0.2 M stock solution, were prepared using double-distilled water. The ZnO/CQDs were dispersed in a water solution and subjected to ultrasonication until complete dispersion was achieved. The luminescent properties of the synthesized ZnO/CQDs in the water solution were examined at room temperature. To investigate the ability of the ZnO/CQDs to sense and detect metal ions, 30 µL of ZnO/CQDs were mixed with Na-Ac buffer solution (0.2 M, pH 7) after adding a series of metal ions. Furthermore, to assess the selectivity of the luminescent ZnO/CQDs in sensing metal ions, luminescence spectra were measured both before and after the addition of other metal ions.

The procedure for Cd2+ sensing using fluorescence quenching of ZnO/CQDs involves the following steps: Step 1: A 0.2 M stock solution of Cd2+ is prepared, which means that a certain amount of Cadmium chloride or another suitable source of Cd2+ is dissolved in water to make a 0.2 M concentration. Step 2: To create a range of concentrations for the Cd2+ ion, working solutions are made by diluting the stock solution. These working solutions cover a concentration range of 0 to 150 µM. The dilution is likely done using a sodium acetate buffer (Na-Ac), which acts as a suitable buffer solution to maintain a constant pH of 7. Step 3: ZnO/CQDs are used as the fluorescent material for the experiment. A volume of 30 µL of the ZnO/CQDs is taken and mixed with the Na-Ac buffer solution (0.2 M, pH 7) to create a uniform mixture. Step 4: Various concentrations of Cd2+ from the working solutions are added to the ZnO/CQDs + Na-Ac buffer mixture prepared in the previous step. By doing so, the Cd2+ ions interact with the ZnO/CQDs, leading to fluorescence quenching. Step 5: After adding Cd2+ to the mixture, it is incubated at room temperature for 3 min. This incubation period allows the Cd2+ ions to bind to the ZnO/CQDs and reach equilibrium. Step 6: Once the incubation period is complete, the fluorescence (FL) signals are measured. The measurement is done at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm. The fluorescence signals are recorded to observe the extent of quenching, which is directly related to the concentration of Cd2+ ions present in the mixture.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cd2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para:
Water samples (drinking water and tap water) that had undergone pre-treatment were chosen in order to assess the viability of this approach towards Cd2+. We received a sample of tap water from our lab, and it was filtered via 0.22 µm filter membrane. From one of Delhi's supermarkets (India), a sample of used drinking water was taken. To ensure that this approach was accurate, the traditional supplemental method was used. Relative recoveries for Cd2+ were determined by spiking 5 and 10 µM using the relation (2):
(2)
R e c o v e r y
%
=
Obtained
value ( μ M )
S p i k e d
v a l u e
μ M
X 100 %

XRD patterns of ZnO and ZnO/CQDs nanomaterials are displayed in Fig. 1 . XRD pattern of the ZnO nanorods exhibits characteristic diffraction peaks at 2θ value of 31.7°, 34.3°, 36.1°, 47.7°, 56.6°, 62.8°, 66.3°, 68.0°, 69.1°, 72.6° and 77.0° that correspond to (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (200), (112), (201), (004) and (202) planes which are matching well with the JCPDS card no.36–1451 and ZnO showing hexagonal structure. The XRD pattern of ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite exhibits similar characteristic diffraction peaks as that of pure ZnO nanorods. No characteristic diffraction peaks of CQDs were observed in the XRD pattern of ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite, which could be attributed to the small amount of CQDs present in the nanocomposite. Using Debye Scherrer formula [21] the average crystallite size is determined. The average crystallite size of ZnO and ZnO/CQDs nanomaterials are found to be 31 nm and 33 nm respectively.

FESEM images of ZnO and ZnO/CQDs nanomaterials are displayed in Fig. 2 (a, c). The FESEM images exhibit that the as-synthesized nanomaterials have a rod like morphology. The mean diameter and mean length of pure ZnO nanorods is ∼ 38.52 nm and 158.6 nm, respectively. However, after compositing CQDs with ZnO nanorods, the mean diameter and mean length of ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite are found to be ∼ 46.08 nm and ∼ 181.3 nm, respectively. The potential growth mechanism for the creation of ZnO nanorods has been extensively reported. The ZnO hexagonal crystal generally has polar and nonpolar faces. It has a positive polar plane that is rich in zinc (0001) and a negative polar plane that is rich in oxygen (000
1
¯
). The planes parallel to the c-axis are nonpolar planes (01
1
¯
0). As the polar planes have a less thermodynamic stability, leading to rapid growth in a specific direction to lower their surface energy, resulting in rod-like structures [22]. The TEM image of as-prepared CQDs show the average particle diameter of 2.30 nm as shown in Fig. 2(e). The size distribution of ZnO, CQDs and ZnO/CQDs nanomaterials are shown in Fig. 2.

FTIR spectra of ZnO and ZnO/CQDs nanomaterials are presented in Fig. 3 . For both the samples, the absorption bands observed at around 3428, 2923, 2850 and 2339 cm−1 are attributed to O–H, C–H stretching vibrations and presence of atmospheric CO2 in the instrument, respectively [23,24]. The FTIR spectrum of the ZnO nanorods reveals four characteristic absorption peaks at around 1625, 1386, 1127 and 528 cm−1, which are ascribed to the C = O carboxyl or hydroxyl groups, C-OH and Zn-O stretching vibrations, respectively [25–28]. The FTIR spectrum of the ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite exhibits characteristic absorption peaks at around 1603, 1357, 1112 and 492 cm−1, which are attributed to the C = C stretching vibration, O-C-O symmetric vibration, C-O and Zn-O stretching vibrations, respectively [28–30]. The C = C stretching vibrations, suggesting the presence of carbon–carbon double bonds, likely associated with the carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The O-C-O symmetric vibrations, possibly indicating the presence of functional groups involving oxygen atoms. The C-O and Zn-O stretching vibrations, respectively, which are characteristic of the composite material, suggesting interactions between ZnO and the carbon quantum dots. The absorption peaks are found to be shifted towards shorter wavelength region in FTIR spectrum of the ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite, confirming that the CQDs have been incorporated in the nanocomposite. Further, the difference in the absorption band intensity of pure ZnO and ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite ensured CQDs are incorporated in the nanocomposite.

XPS measurements are also conducted to further study the bonding, chemical composition and surface state within ZnO and ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), the wide full scan XPS spectrum reveals that the binding energies of Zn 2p, O 1 s, and C 1 s are 1021.28 eV, 531.9 eV, and 284.16 eV, respectively. According to this finding, the primary constituents of as-produced ZnO/CQDs are zinc, oxygen, and carbon. The Zn 2p spectrum exhibits two peaks at 1022.28 and 1045.32 eV, which correspond to Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2, respectively as shown in Fig. 4(b) due to spin orbital splitting indicates that Zn2+ is likely present in the sample [31]. Fig. 4(c) depicts the O1s high resolution spectra, having two peaks at 531.04 eV (O–H/Zn-O/C-Zn-O) and 535.48 eV (C-O) [32,33]. Also, the peaks in the high resolution C1s XPS spectra are found to be at 284.45 eV (C = C/C–C), 284.30 eV (C-N), and 287.15 eV (C = N/C = O), suggesting the presence of CQDs within the composite as shown in Fig. 4(d) [34]. Based on these XPS results, the nanocomposite is composed of zinc (Zn), oxygen (O), and carbon (C). The presence of Zn2+ ions suggests that ZnO is present in the composite. Additionally, the presence of various oxygen and carbon functional groups (such as hydroxyl groups, carbon–oxygen, carbon–nitrogen, and carbon–carbon bonds) indicates the incorporation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) within the ZnO matrix, contributing to the synthesis of the ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite.

The UV–visible absorption spectra of ZnO and ZnO/CQDs nanomaterials are displayed in Fig. 5 (a). The pristine ZnO shows the absorption edge at around 405 nm whereas the ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite exhibited the absorption edge at around 435 nm which is red shifted. A redshift observed in the ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite is attributed to the creation of shallow levels inside the band gap [35]. It has been noted that the absorbance of the ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite has increased even in the visible light range. The increased intensity of visible light absorption is caused by the presence of CQDs on ZnO nanorods, which is ascribed to the rise in the surface electric charge of the oxides in the ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite and the transformation of the basic process of electron–hole pair creation during irradiation [36]. The optical band gaps are estimated by using the Tauc’s relation [21]. The plot of (αhν)2 vs hν for ZnO samples is shown in Fig. 5(b). The band gap values for pure ZnO and ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite are found to be 3.15 eV and 2.89 eV, respectively. The decrease in the band gap value of as-prepared nanocomposite as compared to ZnO may be attributed to the participating Zn in chemical bonding and developing, for instance, a Zn-O-C bond at the defect site [33].

Photoluminescence (PL) is a useful method for identifying defect states in semiconducting materials and provides crucial details about defective emissions. Fig. 6 (a) displays the PL spectra of ZnO and ZnO/CQDs nanostructures at room temperature with an excitation wavelength of 340 nm. The spectrum of ZnO show emission in the near band edge (UV) region at around 395 nm, which can be attributed to the recombination of an electron in the conduction band with a hole in the valence band [37]. The wide visible emission band in ZnO, which spans the wavelengths of 440 to 530 nm, results from electronic transitions between defect states. Generally speaking, for radiative recombination processes in ZnO nanostructures, the most prevalent defect states include anti-sites, vacancies, zinc and oxygen interstitial [38]. As observed in Fig. 6 (a), at 570 nm, the PL emission spectrum of ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite displays red shifted yellowish emission peak with increased intensity compared to that observed in pure ZnO. The maximal interfacial contact between CQDs and ZnO nanorod, which plays a role in the formation of the surface defects and consequently contributes to intense photoluminescence, may be the cause of the luminescence with increased intensity [39]. The red shift is in favour of electron transfer from ZnO nanorod to CQDs [40]. As a result, the interfacial contact is responsible for the electron and energy transfer from ZnO to CQDs, which has an important role in demonstrating the characteristics of ZnO/CQDs nanomaterials. Fig. 6(b) illustrates the photoluminescence spectra of CQDs under different excitations ranging from 280 to 380 nm. As the excitation wavelength increases, the intensity of the emission spectra exhibits an initial increase between 280 and 300 nm, followed by a redshift in the emission with decreasing intensity between 320 and 380 nm. These observations demonstrate the dependence of CQDs' behavior on the excitation conditions. The obtained photoluminescent quantum yields (PLQY) for CQDs, ZnO and ZnO/CQDs are 12.14%, 18.7% and 20.67%, respectively. These results demonstrate that ZnO/CQDs exhibit a higher PLQY compared to other synthesized samples.

PL emission of pure ZnO can be tuned by compositing with CQDs. To describe tunability, a number of colorimetric parameters are determined, including the Comission International de L”Eclairage (CIE) color coordinates, correlated color temperature (CCT) and the color purities (CP). Table 1 , lists several colorimetric parameters of as-prepared ZnO nanorods as well as ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite. Fig. 6(c) displays the CIE chromatic diagram, in which color coordinates of as-synthesized nanomaterials are shown. Color coordinates are found to be in the blue and yellow-orange zones, demonstrating an evolution of color coordinates as a result of compositing and displaying a clear candidate for white light emission when assembled with blue chips. Moreover, color purity of these nanomaterials is calculated using the relation (3) [31]:
(3)
CP =
(
x s
-
x i
)
2
+
(
y s
-
y i
)
2
(
x d
-
x i
)
2
+
(
y d
-
y i
)
2
X 100 %

Where (xi, yi), (xs, ys) and (xd, yd), respectively, constitute the color coordinates of the illuminated point and the sample point, as well as the dominating wavelength. From Table 1, it can be observed that after compositing CQDs with ZnO nanorods, the color purity is found to get enhanced, which illustrates unique colorimetric properties of ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite. Using McCamy's empirical formula (4), the CCTs are determined [31]:
(4)
CCT = - 473
n
3
+ 3601
n
2
- 6861 n + 5514.31

Where, n=(x-xe)/(y-ye) and the chromaticity epicentres are found at xe = 0.3320 and ye = 0.1858. The determined CCT value of ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite is found to be 2513 K as shown in Table 1, indicating their suitability for warm white LEDs [41]. The study of optical characteristics verifies the availability of phosphor suitable for use in the development of white light emitting devices employing blue LED chips.

The photoluminescence of ZnO/CQDs shows a high intensity and broad emission spectrum. A broad emission band is expected to increase the detection concentration range. As a result, ZnO/CQDs could be a good candidate for a fluorescent probe with a broader detection range.

For wide range of applications, fluorescent nanoparticles with great optical stability are useful. The effect of pH on fluorescence intensity of nanocomposite is initially investigated as shown in Fig. 7 (a). Here, F0 denotes the intensity of the blank sample, and F denotes the fluorescence intensity following the addition of external reagents, which in this instance is pH with a variable concentration. Fluorescence intensity shows little change when pH increased from 3 to 12 and finally, we have chosen pH 7 during the entire experiment. Furthermore, a high ionic strength cannot affect the intensity of fluorescence. Fig. 7(b) shows that as the concentration of NaCl was increased to 2.0 M, the fluorescence intensity remained constant. In addition, after 30 days of storage at room temperature, the fluorescence intensity of nanocomposite remained nearly unchanged (Fig. 7(c)). All of these findings suggest that ZnO/CQDs have good optical stability under a variety of experimental conditions, ensuring their use in bio-sensing and environmental applications.

To assess the detection specificity of ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite as a fluorescence sensor, under identical conditions, we added different types of metal interfering ions to the sensing platform. The letters F and F0 stand for the fluorescence intensities of nanocomposite in the presence and absence of interfering ions, respectively. To evaluate the degree to which interfering ions impacted the sensing system and the level of fluorescence quenching of specific responsive ions, we often employed the ratio F/F0, the relative fluorescence intensity. After addition of different metal ion (Fe3+, Cu2+, Bi2+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Sn2+, Ag2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Na+, Sr2+), change in relative fluorescence intensity is investigated. As shown in Fig. 8 , the fluorescence intensity was essentially unaffected by the presence of other metal ions but the addition of Cd2+ causes a significant reduction in the fluorescence of nanocomposite relative to that of the other metal ions, suggesting that the ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite exhibit high selectivity towards Cd2+ over other metal ions. For examining sensitivity, the association between fluorescence intensity of nanocomposite and that after Cd2+ addition to nanocomposite is studied. As displayed in Fig. 9 (a), the fluorescence intensity of nanocomposite rapidly reduced as the Cd2+ concentration increased from 0 to 150 µM and hence shows an extremely high sensitivity for sensing Cd2+. Additionally, a good linear connection with a correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.985) is found in the concentration range 0–60 µM as shown in Fig. 9(b). The expression (5) can be used to calculate the fluorescence quenching efficiency.
(5)
F O
F
- 1 = 0.0067
Cd
2 +
+ 0.0237

The calculated detection limit is determined to be 0.14 µM using the equation s/k = 3, where s stands for standard deviation and k for the slope of the calibration curve. Table 2 giving an account of comparison of Cd2+ detection with earlier works shows that the calculated detection limit is much lower than that has been earlier reported in literature [42–47].

The high binding affinity of Cd2+ ions towards the carboxyl, hydroxyl and amino groups of ZnO/CQDs, compared to other metal ions, can be attributed to the vacant d-orbitals of Cd2+ ions. This selectivity is a result of ZnO/CQDs being able to donate an electron pair to the excited state vacant d-orbitals of Cd2+ ions through a non-radiative electron transfer process, leading to the formation of a surface complex as shown in Fig. 10
[48,49].

Zeta potential analysis can be used to comprehend the interaction between ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite and Cd2+. The nanocomposite has zeta potential values of −13.91 and −2.77 mV, respectively, in the absence and presence of Cd2+ as shown in Fig. 11 (a). The change in zeta potential values indicating a strong electrostatic interaction between Cd2+ and nanocomposite [50]. For further confirmation, the FTIR spectra of ZnO/CQDs in the presence and absence of Cd2+ ions are compared. As shown in Fig. 11(b), the position and intensities of the functional group peaks are changed, which suggests an interaction between of ZnO/CQDs and ZnO/CQDs-Cd2+ nanocomposite [51]. Thus, the existence of functional groups on the surface of ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite results in the energy transfer through hydrogen bonding within the ZnO/CQDs-Cd2+ system, which are primarily responsible for the substantial fluorescence quenching of ZnO/CQDs by Cd2+ [52].

In order to better understand the quenching mechanism, the fluorescence decay lifetimes measurement of ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite are performed in the presence and absence of Cd2+. Fig. 11(c) demonstrates the fluorescence transients of ZnO/CQDs in the presence and absence of Cd2+ ions at 340 nm excitation wavelength. Tri exponential fitting (6) can successfully fit both transients [31].
(6)
N
t
=
B 1
e x p
(
- t
τ 1
)
+
B 2
e x p
(
- t
τ 2
)
+
B 3
e x p
(
- t
τ 3
)

Here, the amplitudes B1, B2, and B3 correspond to the decay lifetimes τ1, τ2 and τ3 respectively. The equation (7) determines the average decay lifetime [31]
(7)
τ
avg
=
B 1
τ
1
2
+
B 2
τ
2
2
+
B 3
τ
3
2
B 1
τ 1
+
B 2
τ 2
+
B 3
τ 3

Before and after adding Cd2+ ions, the average decay lifetime computed for ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite is found to be almost same (1.2 ns). This implies that the quenching mechanism involves static quenching rather than dynamic quenching [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cd2+ in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To establish the practicality of the proposed sensor for real water samples, the reference materials, tap water and drinking water are tested. Table 3 shows that the average recoveries are in the range of 100.5–106%. These findings demonstrate that the sensor has a good sensitivity and reliability for detecting Cd2+ in actual water samples. Also, the relative standard deviation (RSD) is less than 1%, indicating that the developed technique has excellent potential applicability.

In conclusion, this research presents a highly efficient and rapid approach to create nanocomposite-based phosphors with color-tunable fluorescence emission, offering promising applications in various fields. The XRD analysis of the ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite demonstrates its resemblance to pure ZnO nanorods with a hexagonal structure. Additionally, FESEM images confirm the rod-like morphology of the synthesized nanomaterials. Moreover, the band gap values of pure ZnO and ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite are measured at 3.15 eV and 2.89 eV, respectively. Remarkably, under 340 nm excitation wavelength, the nanocomposite-based phosphor exhibits remarkably strong yellow-orange emission, with chromatic color coordinates of (0.51, 0.47) and an impressive color purity (CP) of 94.3%, signifying the high quality of light emission. Furthermore, the ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite proves to be an exceptional chemical sensor for the selective detection of Cd2+ through a quenching process due to its broad emission spectra. The sensitivity of this sensor is highlighted by its low limit of detection (LOD) at 0.14 µM and an effective detection range of 0–150 µM for Cd2+. The fluorescence sensing platform developed in this study also exhibits promising results in real-world sample detection, with good recovery for Cd2+. Additionally, the research findings open new possibilities for the use of the as-produced nanocomposite in white LEDs, as demonstrated by the CIE plot. This exciting advancement holds significant potential for future lighting and display applications, as well as sensing technology. Altogether, the high-performance and versatile properties of the ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite make it a crucial and valuable material for various technological advancements and practical applications.
The authors have not disclosed any funding.

Shafi Ul Islam: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Urosa Latief: Conceptualization, Methodology. Javid Ali: Methodology, Resources. Mohd. Shahid Khan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
